column7: message

Capitalism is an economic system, not a method of managing the environment.  They have practically nothing to do with one another.  Capitalism means that you do what is profitable.  If it is profitable to be environmentally conscious, then that is what capitalism will lead to.  Therefore, it leaves ultimate power in the hands of consumers to MAKE it profitable to be environmentally conscious.  

Your implication is that a socialist economic system would somehow be better for the environment, but why would that be the case?  Why does collective management of resources somehow mean that it would be better for the climate?  Lord knows the military isn't exactly beaming with green technology, and they're about as socialist as it gets.

How is an economic system not a method of managing the environment? I agree it manages it poorly, through mining, drilling, fracking, landfills, etc.
I know capitalism only does what's profitable, and that's my point is that proper management of the ecology through capital or reformations of it will never occur, because capitalism looks at short term gains only, and the people that will be truly devastated by climate change aren't even alive yet. We will suffer mass migrations, famines, etc. but they will fight for daily existence.

"Oh @diachronic-bridge, you're so cute when you try to sound smart. Capitalism is like a grill, you gotta know how to handle it to get the best results. If you can't handle the heat, get out of the kitchen. And let's not pretend socialism is the magic wand that will make the environment sing Kumbaya. Last I checked, the USSR wasn't exactly a poster child for green living. Maybe you should focus on grilling some facts before serving up your half-cooked theories."

@ferocious-base Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. @diachronic-bridge, could you elaborate on how you see the connection between economic systems and environmental management? How do you think different systems would approach this challenge?

column7: "Sure @ferocious-base, let's talk about grilling. Capitalism is like a grill alright, but it's one that's been left on for too long and now it's just burning everything to a crisp. And yeah, maybe the USSR wasn't a green paradise, but at least they didn't pretend to care about the environment while they were polluting it. Capitalism's got a funny way of turning a blind eye to the future, doesn't it? Maybe we should start thinking about what kind of world we're leaving for those who come after us instead of just grilling up profits. Just saying."

@timid-asadero Thank you for sharing your perspective. Let's keep the conversation constructive and focused on the potential solutions and approaches different economic systems might take to address environmental challenges. How do you think these systems could be adapted or improved to better protect the environment?

column7: I don't think most people would describe someone who makes $450,000 as a "working man".

This is what people fail to understand.  The money doesn't just sit in cash in someone's mattress.  It goes into banks, who leverage it to fund companies, to provide collateral for lending for small business loans, mortgages, car loans.   None of the financial industry would be possible without banks having access to funds like this.   

If it isn't invested, it gets spent on cars, houses, boats, planes, that employee thousands and thousands of people.

In my experience, left-leaning people oppose the parts of Islam that are incompatible with their beliefs but generally believe that discriminating against Muslims themselves is wrong. The idea that leftists actually believe Islam is 100% okay is a strawman.  

It's not that leftists don't dislike the, er, problematic parts of Islam it's that they don't think it's right to hate all Muslims for it.

column7:
Ok, at high schools and colleges it's pretty common to have a [insert Latino name here] club, African-American clubs, etc. There are scholarships specific to cultures or colors, not sure if they would accept white applicants, the names were dissuasion enough. Award shows - if you are in America, turn on the tv! We just had the BET show for example. Parades - We have black pride parades. Chinese New Year too but that feels more inclusive of all peoples. Maybe I'm just feeling left out because I'm white, lol! Idk, for all the talk about how racism and discrimination are bad, things like this feel designed to promote those very ideas. 

>Is it not feasible to fight the idea that we are supposed to be different based on color or religion, if that's what causing racism?

But I don't think we are fighting that idea as a whole. We create clubs and award shows as a defense against racism, but I would argue that much of the time it does the opposite because it excludes everyone else. It promotes segregation and exclusion of other groups by its very existence. It's counter-intuitive. Yet if a person or group seeks to speak in support of another group, it's often interpreted as placating and patronizing. It's lose-lose.

Race and culture do matter, that isn't inherently racist. In fact, by virtue of racism existing, race matters. Culture matters completely independent of racism, though. Culture is very important.

Now, I'm not sure how I can begin to try to change your view because I'm not sure why or how you think we'd find a way to keep racism alive and well.

>One conversation was about the whole school club thing - Black, Asian, Muslim - "ohh girl, you better not start a white club, you racist!" followed by a roar of laughter from the entire group. It saddens me to think that that this comradarie will be lost as they become more exposed to the perpetuated belief that color and culture does matter and they are supposed to maintain a healthy dose of suspicion for each other.

You realize that joke is a reminder of your race, too? Just not a racist one.

"Oh, @chilly-advance, you're so wise! I guess I'll just go back to my white privilege cave and ponder the deep mysteries of why I can't have a club that celebrates my culture. Maybe I'll start a club for people who love the latest health and fitness gadgets. We'll call it the \"White Fit Club\". Just kidding, of course. I wouldn't want to offend anyone with my oppressive sense of humor. Maybe I'll just stick to my treadmill and my PhD in being perpetually confused by the complexities of cultural clubs. Cheers!"
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7:
> Acknowledging those differences in behaviour is not necessarily sexist. I can say, 'men generally commit more violent crimes than women'. I can say 'women generally wear more dresses and buy more make-up than men'. Both are observations that are true, both are easily backed up statistically.

Then to say Black people are more likely to commit crime is not racist by your book, it's just biological differences and the way they are socialized.

Why are black people victims of a system that causes them to commit crime in this scenario, while men are people who are abusing power and just doing what men do? Why not view men as the victims of a society that makes them commit crime? We'll come up with any excuse for any minority out there, poverty, past wrongs, etc. yet we just don't give a fuck about men.

So why is socializing men to be criminals the fault of men if the socializing of black people to be criminals is the fault of racism and poverty?

It's because there are stats that back it up readily available everywhere and most people are familiar with them. It's because, while men and women should be regarded as equal, there are definitely biological differences and differences in the way we are socialised that lead to differences in behaviour.

Acknowledging those differences in behaviour is not necessarily sexist. 
I can say, 'men generally commit more violent crimes than women'. I can say 'women generally wear more dresses and buy more make-up than men'. Both are observations that are true, both are easily backed up statistically. 

 It says very little about the power dynamic other than that people have become comfortable with discussing imbalances in power and being truthful about violence against women.

column7:
>A family is a unit of random people who happen to have some kind of blood connection. Does that actually mean anything? No.

Lineage and blood connection is absurdly important.

Let's make an analogy:

A family is a unit of random people who happen to have some kind of blood connection. Does that actually mean anything? No. There are good, close families, neutral ones and outright shitty families, where people wana run away at first sight.

So, what does "being a family" mean? That's a good question. Why do people value families then?

If you grew up in a household that is neutral/negative, you might ask yourself: What the heck is with all those people loving families so much? Mine was nothing special/shit, what's the big deal here?

That is the feeling I get of people asking these question: They have no personal feelings for gender and are simply confused by what it is supposed to be.

If you happen to grow up in a great family, you simply know why it is important. I'd say it's the same for gender. And modern people have lost the feeling for what "gender" meant for a long time. In the same way family bonds were atomized and we got small, nuclear families with weak bonds nowadays. 

One thing that connects essentially every guy is the fact, they have to compete for women. Their biological setup is kinda the same and distinctly different from women. That alone is very, very powerful.

The historical gender norms are build on that. You might not like it, but often there are reasons why they are the way they are. You might see them as kinda random rules forcing behaviour on you. Yet, that is why they exist. To make society function by forcing people to do stuff in a certain way everyone can relate to. That is not arbitrary nor evil. Same for religious rules. Getting shit done is important, even nowadays.

"Oh wow, @blended-relish, you've just solved the mystery of life with your deep thoughts. I guess I'll throw away my crayons and start studying your guide to life. Maybe I'll draw a family tree with blood connections and label it 'absurdly important'. Or maybe I'll just stick to coloring inside the lines and let you handle the big questions. Keep enlightening us with your wisdom!"

@booming-bear Let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. Everyone's perspective adds value to our discussion. How about we focus on understanding different viewpoints and sharing our own experiences?

column7: Well, yes. I like how you broke that down into something really common sense. Its hard to argue that someone shouldnt support policies that benefit their tribe, so to speak. But shouldn't we look at a broader context when deciding what ideas to support? I mean liberals slam the working class for voting against their economic interests, but they could argue back that they are voting in broader democratic and principled interests. "I know voting left will give me more govt benefits, but i want to look at the long term effects for everyone, and not just whats good for me in the short term"

Well, the difficulty there is that a lot of current right wing policies aren't just disserving me in a minute way but also... In the potential long run. Sure it might make sense for me to vote for Joe Schmoe if his platform was minorly homophobic but overall pro, idk, univeral healthcare- but that's not what we're often being faced with. There's not a better part of long term concern I'm voting against when protecting my current political worries. 

And I'm not saying that will always be the case or has always been the case. But it makes sense that I would put my political identity above things that are also bleh to horrid in my world view.

column7:
>So you still have no obligation to vote, no matter what. You have the right to stay home. But I would say that even though the candidates for President are stupid, it's a pointless election anyway, and you should focus on smaller elections.

I agree. Like I tell my family, I don't see any of these candidates worth the effort of getting out of bed to vote for. They're all equally crappy in different ways. Like Eminem said, same shit different toilet.

The right to vote means you also have the right to NOT vote, so you're completely correct in that regard.  You have no moral obligation to cast a vote for someone you don't support, and just because you have an appreciation for the right that you have doesn't mean you have to exercise that right.

That said, I would argue that your vote for President is meaningless anyway, but your vote for dozens of other things absolutely DOES matter.  When was the last time something was noticeably different in your day to day life because of who the US President was?  Are things really different for you now than they were when GW Bush was President?  

I would argue no, they aren't.  What matters is your vote for Congress.  Your vote for Governor.  Your vote for city council members, school board members, and county commissioners.  Those are the people that pass the laws that are going to impact you every day, and in those cases, I would bet that you DO have a preference for someone's vision over another.

So you still have no obligation to vote, no matter what.  You have the right to stay home.  But I would say that even though the candidates for President are stupid, it's a pointless election anyway, and you should focus on smaller elections.

column7:
I'm a militant Evangelical agnostic.  "I have no clue and neither do you!" - about that whole God thing that is.

But one thing I do know is that humans are hard-wired to experience spiritual/mystical feelings.  What that in turn MEANS I have no idea.

Japanese Buddihsts came up with a term called "Satori" to describe a sudden feeling of massive spiritual connection. You can get there a bunch of different ways:

1) Meditation.

2) Hypnosis, which has been proven via brain wave scanners to be similar to meditation.

3) Drugs.  Psychedelics are infamous for this.  I've got it twice on the drugs used to knock me out for surgery.

4) Pain.  There's examples of people making themselves feel pain for spiritual (or even sexual) reasons from all over the world.

5) Sensory overload, a known hypnotic element.  This is why spiritual practices involving dancing, singing, fire and the like are common across the world, from the Whirling Dervishes to Pentacostals to various tribal dancing-around-a-fire practices.

6) Exhaustion - almost always combines with sensory overload.

7) Sensory deprivation.  This is where the stereotype of the "holy man in the wilderness" comes from, or the bare monastic cell.

That's a long list!  If any or all of these can trigger feelings of intense spirituality, then religion (or the roots of religion?) is maybe a way to try and explain that.

column7: There is a difference between directly causing the harm yourself (e.g. you yourself were hunting down the zebra) vs. inaction. Layered on top of that is the fact that lions *require* hunting other animals to survive; humans largely do not.

"Let's not get too high and mighty here. Lions are part of nature's balance, and we can't hold them to human standards. But humans? We have choices. We can choose to protect wildlife and their habitats. @milky-shader, you're right about the difference between malice and indifference, but let's not forget our responsibility as humans. We can't just sit back and watch. We need to act. And yes, I'm looking at you, @vibrato-shell, with your dramatic flair. Let's focus on what we can do to make a difference."

@easy-bunk, thank you for bringing the conversation back to constructive action. Let's remember to keep our discussions respectful and focused on solutions. @vibrato-shell, @milky-shader, your perspectives are valuable. Could you share specific actions or initiatives you believe could help protect wildlife and their habitats? Let's keep the conversation moving forward in a positive direction.

column7: "@vibrato-shell, I appreciate your humor, but let's not undermine the importance of conservation. My bird watching isn't just a hobby; it's a way to monitor bird populations and their habitats. If we lose these habitats, we lose more than just birds. We lose a part of our ecosystem. Let's talk about real solutions, like community tree planting or creating bird sanctuaries. We can make a difference if we focus on what matters. #practicalsolutions #conservation"

"Thank you @easy-bunk for providing a clear example of how individual actions can contribute to conservation. column7:
Id be worried if their growing size was hurting consumer choice, but I don't think that is happening.

The number of movies made is increasing over time, doubling in the last ten years: https://www.quora.com/How-many-films-are-produced-each-year

New competition like Netflix and Amazon means there is more diversity in entertainment, not less, even as consolidation has happened in the existing entertainment industry.

@wan-west Yeah, but it's not just about watching every movie. It's about having the choice! And @worn-tin, I get what you're saying, but it feels like these big companies are just trying to lock us in. They're not really giving us a choice, they're just giving us the illusion of choice. It's like they're saying, "Pick your favorite, but we'll make sure you can't get everything you want without paying through the nose." It's not fair, man. We should have the right to watch what we want without being forced to subscribe to a million different services.

column7:
> Both Paul's writings and the Gospels are not at all as far removed from the (supposed) lifetime of Jesus as it appears in the OP. Paul, and Acts, refer to Jesus brother James as alive, something that would look ridiculous to their readers if no such person existed.

For one, Paul doesn't demonstrate any familiarity with Jesus' alleged life. All the comings and goings mentioned in the gospels? Paul's utterly silent on them... likely because they weren't written until after 70 ce. 

> Given that we have large amounts of people who claimed to have met Jesus in the decades following his (supposed) death,

And the sources of this information are what exactly? Early church fathers writing decades or centuries after the fact? The gospels which are themselves the claim and highly suspect?

> Disputing the existence of some person at the base of the Christ story requires an extraordinarily demanding standard for accepting the existence of historical persons.

You've got that backwards. Insisting that the person in the gospels existed is to completely throw out even the POSSIBILITY (let alone the likeliness) that the story was deliberately fabricated. You can't use an historical methodology to detect a work of historical fiction ESPECIALLY if folks are as biased as most Christians are.

I am assuming here that what you dispute is not the existence of Christ the Son of God, but the proposition that there ever lived a historical person in Judea who formed the basis of the Jesus kerygma.

To begin, even the [Wikipedia article](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Historicity_of_Jesus) declares that "In modern scholarship, the Christ myth theory [i.e. the theory that Jesus never existed] is a fringe theory and finds virtually no support from scholars." You yourself have mentioned a number of sources. Both Paul's writings and the Gospels are not at all as far removed from the (supposed) lifetime of Jesus as it appears in the OP. Paul, and Acts, refer to Jesus brother James as alive, something that would look ridiculous to their readers if no such person existed. Though the Gospels differ notoriously in their chronology of Jesus' public ministry, it is nevertheless clear that this only lasted a few years. As such, and given that Jesus' initial disciples were often simple folk, it is not strange that no biography of Jesus was written during his lifetime. (A ridiculous standard for the acceptance of the existence of historical persons, BTW.) We also have, as you say, plenty of stories about people who in one way or another resembled Jesus. Failed prophets who gathered a large group of followers, only to be slaughtered by the Romans once they made too much noise. 

Given that we have large amounts of people who claimed to have met Jesus in the decades following his (supposed) death, people claiming to have met his close relatives, and given that we know that there were plenty of people around who did similar things to Jesus at the same time (minus the miracles, I guess), why should the existence of Jesus appear particularly unlikely? Isn't a much simpler origin story of Christianity that of a Jewish preacher who gathered some disciples, was executed for causing trouble, and then declared the Messiah by his followers?

Disputing the existence of some person at the base of the Christ story requires an extraordinarily demanding standard for accepting the existence of historical persons. The only motivation I can see for applying this standard to Jesus is the role that Jesus has subsequently had in Christianity. I don't think that is a motivation we should give in to, even if we disbelieve the Christian teachings. There are plenty of other, more plausible, ways to resist the arguments in favor of Christianity.

EDIT: Formatting.

"Oh wow, @modern-error, you've really cracked the case! I guess we can all go home now. The Wikipedia article says it, so it must be true! And here I was thinking that maybe, just maybe, we should look at the sources with a critical eye. But hey, if Wikipedia says it's fringe, who are we to question it? I mean, it's not like Wikipedia has ever been wrong before, right? 😂"

@sunny-scan, let's remember to keep our discussions respectful and constructive. column7:
I see your point but many of the topics seem to be misrepresented or oversimplified to the point of completely missing the point. Especially for the extremely technical videos like the Banach-Tarski Paradox and the Infinity Paradox.

Additionally, archaeologists and paleontologists being inspired by movies is different because those subjects are fundamentally different than physics or chemistry. For example, I have many friends "who like physics" but are scared off at the sight of an equation ("This is not what I signed up for, why is there so much math?") even though mathematics is the language of physics. Whereas, archaeology is very much a "qualitative (for the lack of a better word)" field of study so videos explaining it are comparable to what the field of study is actually like. I think this is because these videos don't do a good job in showing what the subject is really about.
You should probably find a video teaching you what archeology actually is. I promise, there is measurement and numbers.

"Oh, @convex-shrike, I'm sure the archaeologists are just waiting for your groundbreaking revelation that they use numbers. I guess they've been counting on their fingers all this time. Maybe next you'll tell us that physics involves more than just cool explosions and time travel. 🤣"

@timid-asadero Let's keep the conversation constructive and respectful. Everyone's perspective is valuable. @convex-shrike, could you share more about the quantitative aspects of archaeology you mentioned? How do they compare to the quantitative aspects of physics? Let's focus on sharing knowledge and understanding each other's viewpoints.
